office-hours

Simulates a YC partner session, stress-testing your product direction with hard questions before you write a single line of code
1825 lines · 6-phase branching flow · v2.0.0 · gstack ecosystem

Lines
6+
Phases
2
Branch Paths
42
Code Blocks

External URLs
8
Tool Permissions

01Overview

Two modes, one core pipeline. Depending on whether you're building a startup or a side project, you get a different question track, but both converge into the same design document.
flowchart TB
  P1["Phase 1
Context Gathering"]
  MODE{"Startup or
Side Project?"}
  P2A["Phase 2A
Startup Diagnostic
6 Forcing Questions"]
  P2B["Phase 2B
Builder Co-creation
5 Generative Questions"]
  P25["Phase 2.5
Prior Design Discovery"]
  P275["Phase 2.75
Landscape Awareness"]
  P3["Phase 3
Premise Challenge"]
  P35["Phase 3.5
Cross-Model 2nd Opinion"]
  P4["Phase 4
Alternatives Generation"]
  P45["Phase 4.5
Founder Signal Synthesis"]
  P5["Phase 5
Design Document"]
  SR["Spec Review Loop
Adversarial Review"]
  P6["Phase 6
Handoff + Resources"]

  P1 --> MODE
  MODE -->|"Startup / Intrapreneurship"| P2A
  MODE -->|"Hackathon / OSS / Learning / Fun"| P2B
  P2A --> P25
  P2B --> P25
  P25 --> P275
  P275 --> P3
  P3 --> P35
  P35 --> P4
  P4 --> P45
  P45 --> P5
  P5 --> SR
  SR --> P6

Two Modes

  • Startup Mode STARTUP — Simulates a YC partner diagnostic. Six "forcing questions" designed to expose whether demand is real, find your narrowest wedge, and kill bad ideas early.
  • Builder Mode BUILDER — Acts as an enthusiastic collaborator, not an interrogator. Generative questions help you find the most exciting version of your project.

Hard Constraint

This skill only produces design documents. It will never write code, scaffold projects, or take any implementation action. It asks one question at a time and waits for your answer before continuing.

02Walkthrough

The full flow from context gathering to design document.
gstack framework initialization: version checks, telemetry setup, config loading, session management, learnings retrieval, and other shared infrastructure (~600 lines of framework code).Source: lines 29-598

Phase 1: Context Gathering BOTH

Understand the project before asking anything. Reads CLAUDE.md and TODOS.md for project context, checks git log for recent activity, and uses Grep/Glob to map the codebase. If prior office-hours sessions exist, it surfaces those design docs. Then asks the key question: what's your goal? The answer determines whether you enter Startup Mode or Builder Mode.
Input
Project codebase + user-stated goal
Output
Project understanding summary + mode selection (Startup / Builder)
Prior Learnings: loads insights from previous sessions stored in ~/.gstack/. On first use, asks whether to enable cross-project knowledge sharing.Source: lines 624-687

Phase 2A: Startup Diagnostic — 6 Forcing Questions STARTUP

Simulates a YC partner meeting. Asks six high-pressure questions one at a time, pushing on each until you give specific, evidence-based answers. The goal isn't to be difficult — it's to catch directional mistakes before you invest months building the wrong thing.
#QuestionPush Until You Hear
Q1Demand Reality — What evidence do you have that someone actually wants this?Specific behavior: someone paying, expanding usage, or dependent on your product
Q2Status Quo — How are users solving this problem today, even badly?A concrete workflow: hours spent, dollars wasted, tools duct-taped together
Q3Desperate Specificity — Name the actual human who needs this most.A name, title, KPI, and what keeps them up at night
Q4Narrowest Wedge — What's the smallest version someone would pay for this week?One feature, one workflow, shippable in days not months
Q5Observation & Surprise — Have you watched someone use this? What surprised you?A specific surprise — users doing something the product wasn't designed for
Q6Future-Fit — In 3 years, does your product become more essential or less?A specific thesis about how the world changes in your favor

Smart routing: Not everyone gets all six. Pre-product gets Q1/Q2/Q3, has users gets Q2/Q4/Q5, has revenue gets Q4/Q5/Q6.
Escape hatch: If you're impatient, it asks at most 2 more critical questions then moves on.

Phase 2B: Builder Co-creation BUILDER

A completely different vibe from Startup Mode. No interrogation — it's your collaborator. Five generative questions help you explore the most exciting possibilities: What's the coolest version? Who would you show it to? What's the fastest path to something you can use or share?
Input
Your project idea
Output
Refined direction + clear definition of what makes it cool

If you mention customers, revenue, or fundraising mid-conversation, it automatically upgrades to Startup Mode.

Phase 2.5: Prior Design Discovery BOTH

Searches ~/.gstack/projects/ for existing design docs with keyword overlap. If a previous office-hours session covered related ground, it surfaces the doc and asks whether to build on it or start fresh.
Input
Keywords from current problem statement
Output
Related prior designs (if any)

Phase 2.75: Landscape Awareness BOTH

Searches the outside world for what people think about your space, but only uses generalized category terms — never your specific idea. Results get digested in three layers: what everyone knows, what's being discussed now, and whether conventional wisdom contradicts what came up in your conversation. If it does, that gets flagged as a EUREKA moment.
Input
Problem domain (generalized terms)
Output
Three-layer analysis + potential EUREKA insights

Asks for consent before searching. You can decline and proceed with the model's existing knowledge only.

Phase 3: Premise Challenge BOTH

Before proposing solutions, challenge the problem itself. Maybe you've framed it wrong. Maybe doing nothing is the best answer. Maybe existing code already solves half of it. Lists 3-5 premises for you to confirm or reject. Any rejected premise means going back to revise direction.
Input
Phase 2 diagnostic conclusions
Output
Confirmed premise list

Phase 3.5: Cross-Model Second Opinion BOTH (optional)

Takes a structured summary of everything discussed so far and hands it to an independent AI that hasn't seen the conversation. It gives a "cold read" — identifying the strongest product direction, the most revealing user answer, and which premise it thinks might be wrong. Two AIs that can't see each other's reasoning, cross-checking each other.
Input
Structured summary of Phases 1-3
Output
Independent cold read + cross-model comparison

Prefers Codex (OpenAI); falls back to a Claude subagent if unavailable. You can skip this entirely.

Phase 4: Alternatives Generation (mandatory) BOTH

Produces 2-3 distinct implementation approaches. One "minimal viable" (ships fastest), one "ideal architecture" (best long-term), and optionally one "lateral" (reframes the problem entirely). Each approach includes effort estimate, risk level, and what existing code it reuses.
Input
Confirmed premises + second opinion
Output
2-3 approach comparison + recommendation
Visual Design Exploration: if the chosen approach involves UI, can generate 3 style variants using a design binary and present a comparison board. Falls back to HTML wireframe when the binary isn't available.Source: lines 1099-1262
Phase 4.5 Founder Signal Synthesis: reviews the entire conversation for "founder traits" — naming specific users, pushing back on premises, showing domain expertise, demonstrating taste. Signal strength determines how strong the recommendation is in the closing phase.Source: lines 1263-1307

Phase 5: Design Document BOTH

Converges everything into a structured markdown design doc, saved to ~/.gstack/projects/{slug}/. Startup and Builder modes use different templates, but both include problem statement, premises, approach comparison, recommended approach, and success criteria. The doc ends with "What I noticed about how you think" — quoting your own words back as feedback.
Input
All phase outputs
Output
~/.gstack/projects/{slug}/{user}-{branch}-design-{datetime}.md

Spec Review Loop (adversarial review) BOTH

After writing the design doc, dispatches an independent AI reviewer that hasn't seen the conversation. It reviews the document on five dimensions — completeness, consistency, clarity, scope, and feasibility — and scores it. Issues get fixed, then reviewed again, up to three rounds.
Input
Draft design document
Output
Reviewed document + quality score

Phase 6: Handoff — The Relationship Closing BOTH

Once the design doc is approved, delivers a three-beat closing. Beat 1 quotes your own words back, reflecting on the thinking you demonstrated. Beat 2 shifts to a "Garry Tan personal note" mode, with YC recommendation calibrated to your founder signal strength. Beat 3 shares 2-3 curated resources from a pool of 34 (YC videos, Paul Graham essays, etc.) with automatic dedup so returning users never see the same link twice.
Input
Approved design doc + founder signal assessment
Output
Personalized closing + resource recommendations + next-skill suggestions
Capture Learnings: before the session ends, records valuable discoveries (patterns, pitfalls, user preferences, architectural decisions) to learnings.jsonl for automatic reuse in future sessions.Source: lines 1791-1815

03Security Scan

Trust report based on the OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications (2026) framework.

Medium Risk

🌐
Phase 2.75 performs web searches for industry landscape
consent: asks via AskUserQuestion before searching, user can decline · search terms are generalized, never include your specific product details
line 910
🤖
Phase 3.5 sends a structured summary to Codex (OpenAI) or a Claude subagent
consent: asks via AskUserQuestion, user can decline · sends structured summary, not raw conversation · external AI has no conversation context
line 963
📁
Writes multiple files under ~/.gstack/
design docs, analytics JSONL, learnings, resource-shown logs, config · 58 file operations, all confined to user config directory
various
⚙️
Invokes multiple gstack ecosystem binaries
gstack-config, gstack-telemetry-log, gstack-learnings-search, gstack-slug, gstack-timeline-log etc. · purpose: config read/write, local telemetry, experience retrieval
Preamble
📊
Local telemetry with optional remote reporting
local JSONL always written to ~/.gstack/analytics/ · remote reporting only after user opts into community/anonymous mode via gstack-telemetry-log binary · fully disableable
line 450
🔗
Phase 6 may open URLs via the open command
targets: ycombinator.com/apply and YouTube/Paul Graham resource links · consent: AskUserQuestion lets user choose whether to open
line 1534
Summary: All network operations (web search, cross-model calls, URL opening) are gated behind explicit user consent and can be declined individually. File operations are confined to ~/.gstack/. Telemetry has a three-level switch (community/anonymous/off). No toxic flows detected, no prompt injection found. Rated medium because it sends a summary of your discussion to an external AI.

04Context

Ecosystem: gstack ▶

office-hours is part of the gstack skill suite. gstack provides a shared preamble (~600 lines covering version checks, telemetry, config, session management, and a learnings system) that all gstack skills inherit.

Downstream skills ▶
  • /plan-ceo-review — high-level strategy review (for ambitious plans)
  • /plan-eng-review — architecture and test review (locks down implementation)
  • /plan-design-review — visual and interaction review
External tool dependencies ▶
  • codex — OpenAI Codex CLI, used in Phase 3.5 for cross-model second opinion (optional; falls back to Claude subagent)
  • design binary — gstack's design generation tool for visual mockup exploration (optional; falls back to HTML wireframe)
  • gstack-* series — config, telemetry, learnings, timeline, and other gstack infrastructure binaries
Resource pool (34 external links) ▶

Phase 6 picks 2-3 resources matching your session context from this pool. Built-in dedup ensures returning users never see the same link twice.

  • Garry Tan Videos — 5 (incl. "My $200M Startup Mistake", "The New Way To Build A Startup")
  • YC Backstory / How to Build the Future — 2 (Tom Blomfield, DoorDash CEO)
  • Lightcone Podcast — 9 (incl. "Vertical AI Agents 10X Bigger Than SaaS", "Vibe Coding Is The Future")
  • YC Startup School — 8 (incl. "How to Get Startup Ideas", "How To Talk To Users")
  • Paul Graham Essays — 10 (incl. "How to Do Great Work", "Bus Ticket Theory of Genius")
Companion files ▶

Single SKILL.md (1825 lines), auto-generated from SKILL.md.tmpl via gstack's preamble injection mechanism. No standalone bin/ or assets/ directory.

skill-to-html   Built by roxor · 2026
